sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A PET radiotracer sample had an initial activity of 80 MBq and a physical half-life of 6.0 hours. A technologist recorded activity over 24 hours while the sample remained sealed. The activity followed first-order nuclear decay, with activity proportional to the number of radioactive nuclei remaining. After 6 hours the activity was 40 MBq, after 12 hours it was 20 MBq, and after 24 hours it was 5 MBq.
questions.stem: How much activity remains after 12 hours?

explanations.B: Correct. Twelve hours equals two half-lives. Starting from 80 MBq, one half-life gives 40 MBq and two half-lives gives 20 MBq.
explanations.A: 40 MBq remains after one half-life, or 6 hours.
explanations.C: 10 MBq remains after three half-lives, or 18 hours.
explanations.D: Radioactive decay approaches zero but is not exactly zero after two half-lives.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Activity of a radiotracer sample with a 6.0-hour half-life over 24 hours.

questions.stem: What fraction of the original activity remains after 24 hours?

explanations.C: Correct. Twenty-four hours is four half-lives of 6 hours each. After four half-lives, the remaining fraction is (1/2)^4 = 1/16, which corresponds to 5 MBq from an initial 80 MBq.

questions.stem: Why is radioactive decay modeled as first-order?

choices.A: The decay rate is proportional to the number of radioactive nuclei present.
choices.B: The half-life increases every time half the sample decays.
choices.C: Decay requires collision between two nuclei.
choices.D: Activity is independent of the number of nuclei.

explanations.A: Correct. For first-order decay, each nucleus has the same probability of decaying per unit time, so the total activity is proportional to how many radioactive nuclei remain. This produces a constant half-life.
explanations.B: A constant half-life means the half-life does not increase as the sample decays.
explanations.C: Nuclear decay is spontaneous for unstable nuclei and does not require intermolecular collisions.
explanations.D: Activity decreases as the number of radioactive nuclei decreases.

questions.stem: If the initial activity were 160 MBq instead of 80 MBq, what would the activity be after 6 hours?

explanations.D: Correct. After one half-life, half of any initial activity remains. Half of 160 MBq is 80 MBq.
explanations.A: 160 MBq would mean no decay occurred.
explanations.B: 40 MBq would be half of the original 80 MBq sample, not 160 MBq.
explanations.C: 20 MBq would correspond to several half-lives for a 160 MBq sample.

questions.stem: Which type of radiation is used in PET imaging?

choices.C: Positron emission followed by annihilation photons
choices.A: Alpha particles traveling directly to the detector
choices.D: Visible light emitted by electron transitions only

explanations.C: Correct. PET uses positron-emitting radionuclides. The emitted positron annihilates with an electron, producing pairs of gamma photons detected by the scanner. That physical process is distinct from alpha decay or ordinary visible emission.
explanations.A: Alpha particles are not the detected signal in PET and have very short tissue ranges.
explanations.B: Stable carbon-12 does not emit neutrons for PET imaging.
explanations.D: Visible electronic emission is not the basis of PET detection.

questions.passage: A pharmacy prepared aqueous solutions to compare osmotic pressure at 37°C. Solution A contained 0.150 M glucose, a nonelectrolyte. Solution B contained 0.150 M NaCl and was assumed to dissociate ideally into Na+ and Cl-. Osmotic pressure was estimated by π = iMRT. The instructor emphasized that solutions with higher effective particle concentrations draw more water across semipermeable membranes.
questions.stem: Which solution has the higher ideal osmotic pressure?

choices.C: Both are equal because molarity is equal

explanations.B: Correct. NaCl ideally dissociates into two particles per formula unit, so i ≈ 2. Glucose remains one particle, so i = 1. At the same molarity and temperature, NaCl has roughly twice the osmotic pressure.
explanations.A: Glucose is a nonelectrolyte and produces fewer solute particles at the same molarity.
explanations.C: Equal molarity does not mean equal osmolarity when one solute dissociates.
explanations.D: Solute particles in water exert osmotic pressure across semipermeable membranes.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Osmotic pressure estimates using π = iMRT for glucose and ideal NaCl solutions.

questions.stem: What is the van't Hoff factor for ideal NaCl in this passage?

explanations.D: Correct. Ideal NaCl dissociates into Na+ and Cl-, giving two dissolved particles per formula unit. Thus the van't Hoff factor is approximately 2.
explanations.A: A factor of 1 describes a nonelectrolyte such as glucose.
explanations.B: 0.5 would imply fewer particles than formula units, not dissociation.
explanations.C: 150 confuses molarity units with particle count.

questions.stem: Why does osmotic pressure depend on particle concentration rather than solute identity alone?

choices.A: Osmosis is a colligative property determined by the number of dissolved particles.
choices.B: Only the mass of each molecule matters.
choices.C: Only charged solutes can create osmotic pressure.

explanations.A: Correct. Colligative properties depend primarily on how many solute particles are present, not their detailed chemical identity. Dissociation increases particle count and therefore raises osmotic pressure.
explanations.B: Molar mass can affect conversions, but osmotic pressure depends on particle number in solution.
explanations.C: Nonelectrolytes also create osmotic pressure; glucose is the example here.
explanations.D: Water movement can be affected by nonelectrolyte solutes across membranes.

questions.stem: What would happen to osmotic pressure if glucose concentration doubled?

explanations.B: Correct. For a fixed temperature and van't Hoff factor, π is directly proportional to molarity. Doubling glucose concentration doubles the effective particle concentration and therefore doubles π.
explanations.A: Halving would occur if concentration were cut in half.
explanations.C: The equation shows direct concentration dependence.
explanations.D: Osmotic pressure is positive for these solutions.

questions.stem: Why are osmotic differences clinically important for IV fluids?

choices.C: They can drive water movement into or out of cells.
choices.A: They change nuclear half-life of solutes.
choices.B: They make all ions covalently bond to membranes.
choices.D: They eliminate the need for concentration units.

explanations.C: Correct. Cell membranes are semipermeable, so differences in effective solute concentration can shift water across membranes. Hypertonic or hypotonic fluids can shrink or swell cells, which is why osmolarity matters clinically.
explanations.A: Osmotic pressure has no effect on nuclear half-life.
explanations.B: Osmosis does not require ions to covalently bond to membranes.
explanations.D: Clinical fluid preparation depends heavily on concentration units and osmolarity.
